# Solo Developer Architecture Decisions – A Practical Checklist (2024–2026)

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

The table below contrasts **solo-developer defaults** versus **enterprise patterns** that are often **overkill** for one-person projects. Each row is a decision area with the recommended choice, the over-engineered alternative to avoid, why the heavyweight choice hurts solos, and quick heuristics to determine which path fits your situation (indie SaaS or internal prototype):

| **Decision Area**                    | **Solo Dev Default** (Safe Choice)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | **Enterprise/Team Choice** (Often Overkill Solo)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | **Why the Heavy Choice Hurts Solo** (Constraint)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | **Heuristics – When to Stick Solo vs Upgrade**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Database**                         | **Single relational DB** (e.g. one PostgreSQL instance; use SQLite for tiny apps) [\[techbuddies.io\]](https://www.techbuddies.io/2026/01/26/inside-openais-postgresql-strategy-scaling-a-single-primary-to-800-million-users/), [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749). Benefits: ACID transactions, rich queries, low ops overhead. Scale vertically or via read replicas as needed.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | **Distributed / NoSQL clusters** (e.g. Cassandra, Mongo with sharding, multi-DB polyglot). Often adopted “for future scale” unnecessarily [\[techbuddies.io\]](https://www.techbuddies.io/2026/01/26/inside-openais-postgresql-strategy-scaling-a-single-primary-to-800-million-users/).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | *Solo Constraint:* High ops and complexity. Distributed DBs require careful shard logic, replication, and eventual consistency handling – too much for one dev. A single optimized DB can handle millions of rows and high QPS with less effort [\[techbuddies.io\]](https://www.techbuddies.io/2026/01/26/inside-openais-postgresql-strategy-scaling-a-single-primary-to-800-million-users/). Extra databases = extra cognitive load and risk of inconsistency.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | **Heuristics:** If your data fits on one server (e.g. <100M rows or <1TB, under thousands of ops/sec) and doesn’t demand multi-region writes, stick to one DB. Only consider sharding/NoSQL when you physically exhaust a single node’s capacity or have domain-specific need (e.g. document queries that SQL can’t do well). For early products and internal tools, one well-chosen database is almost always enough.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **Service Architecture**             | **Modular Monolith** (one deployable app with clear internal modules) [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html). Keep everything in one codebase/process initially – function calls over network calls. Possibly separate frontend/UI from backend, but keep backend as a single service.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | **Microservices & multiple APIs** (each feature as its own service, independent deploys). Often done by habit from big companies, but leads to a suite of services for a small scope [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html), [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | *Solo Constraint:* Microservices bring a **“microservice premium”** – significant overhead in DevOps and integration [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html). You’d juggle multiple deployments, service discovery, and inter-service bugs alone. Debugging and changing features slows to a crawl with many small services. One dev cannot play the role of a full SRE team for dozens of services.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | **Heuristics:** Start monolithic **unless** you have a proven need for independent scaling or deployment of components (rare for v1.0) [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html). If you’re the only developer (or a tiny team) and the codebase is <100k lines, a monolith is ideal. Use microservices only when modules are so large and decoupled that splitting helps (e.g. after achieving product-market fit and hiring more devs per area). For an internal tool used by one department, microservices are overkill – stick to one service.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| **Deployment Target**                | **Single server or PaaS**. Use one VPS (DigitalOcean, Lightsail, etc.) or a platform like Heroku/Fly.io/Render for web apps. Containerize your app if needed, but run it directly (e.g. Docker Compose on one machine, or a simple Cloud Run container) [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749), [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749). Leverage managed services (DBaaS, etc.) to avoid ops.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | **Kubernetes & complex infra**. E.g. a full **K8s cluster** with Helm charts, service mesh, Canary deploys – meant for multi-service, multi-team scenarios. Also includes multi-zone load balancers, on-prem clusters, etc.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | *Solo Constraint:* Extremely high ops burden for minimal gain. K8s and similar add **YAML complexity, resource overhead, and constant maintenance** [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749). One dev can deploy faster and more cheaply to a single VM than manage a cluster (which might consume hours weekly in upkeep). You’ll spend time on ingress configs and pod debugging instead of building features. Costs are higher too – a K8s control plane or extra nodes idle away budget [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749). In short, you’re solving scale problems you don’t have, with a steep learning curve you must climb alone.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | **Heuristics:** If your entire app fits on one machine (and one replica can handle the load), you don’t need Kubernetes [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749). Use PaaS or simple servers until you have at least a few distinct services *and* traffic in the thousands of reqs/sec range or unpredictable spikes. Consider K8s only when manual management truly fails (e.g. >5-10 servers or services). For prototypes and new SaaS with <1000 daily users, K8s will slow you down – revisit it later if growth demands.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **API Design**                       | **Simple API/Integration**. Use RESTful HTTP endpoints or a direct function call. Design minimal endpoints that exactly serve the UI or task. Embrace monolith-internal calls (no need to force your own API between modules). If only one client (e.g. your React app) consumes it, tailor the API to that client.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | **Over-generalized APIs** – e.g. **GraphQL or gRPC micro-APIs** for every interaction, or an elaborate versioned REST API for a single consumer. Also, adding an API gateway or layer of indirection “just in case” future clients appear.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | *Solo Constraint:* Added layers mean more code and bugs. GraphQL servers require defining schemas, resolvers, and cache handling – a lot of work if you control both client and server. gRPC/Protobuf implies maintaining IDL files and services, which is overkill if internal or one-front-end use. For one dev, simpler is easier to test and document. Overbuilt APIs lead to confusion (e.g. unnecessary flexible queries that you then have to secure and validate).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | **Heuristics:** If you have exactly one primary UI or client and a straightforward use case, go with a REST/JSON API (or no external API at all if front and back are in one project). Only consider GraphQL if you expect many third-party consumers or truly variable query needs. Use gRPC only for internal service-to-service communication when you’ve split services for valid reasons. In early stages, avoid “API-first” complexity – you can always add GraphQL or official SDKs once multiple external teams integrate with your system.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| **Authentication**                   | **Built-in or Simple Auth**. Use framework-provided auth or a lightweight service: e.g. Devise (Rails) or Django Auth for custom accounts, Firebase Auth/Supabase for user management, or Google/Microsoft OAuth for internal tools (so users use their existing credentials). Keep the scope to basic login, roles, and perhaps JWTs for API tokens – nothing fancy.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | **Enterprise IAM** – e.g. deploying **Keycloak, Auth0 enterprise**, custom OAuth2 servers, or complex multi-tenant SSO from scratch. These provide SSO, OIDC flows, user federation – powerful but heavy when you have few users.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | *Solo Constraint:* Running an auth server is a job of its own – user directories, token lifecycles, certificate rotation, etc. For one dev, this is unnecessary toil. Tools like Keycloak consume significant memory and require constant updates. Overbuilt auth also confuses users (e.g. why go through an OAuth dance for a tool used by 5 people?). Simpler methods are easier to secure initially; complex systems, ironically, can introduce security gaps if misconfigured.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | **Heuristics:** Count your users and clients: if it’s just internal staff or early adopters in double-digits, you don’t need full OAuth/OIDC infrastructure. Use built-in username/password or even a shared password for tiny internal apps. If you anticipate enterprise customers who demand single sign-on, you can integrate a managed solution when that time comes. Until you have hundreds of users or multiple apps to unify, avoid deploying a standalone IAM server – it’s overkill.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **Testing Strategy**                 | **Targeted “Risk-Based” Tests**. Focus on high-risk functionality: e.g. a happy-path integration test for core user flows, unit tests for critical calculations [\[ruchitsuthar.com\]](https://ruchitsuthar.com/blog/software-craftsmanship/testing-strategies-startup-to-enterprise), [\[ruchitsuthar.com\]](https://ruchitsuthar.com/blog/software-craftsmanship/testing-strategies-startup-to-enterprise). Use manual testing and monitoring for less critical parts. Prioritize speed of iteration; use tests as safety nets where breakage would be catastrophic (payments, data loss, etc.). For internal tools, you might rely mostly on manual QA since users are in-house.                                                                                                                                                                                          | **Comprehensive Automated Testing** – e.g. 90%+ coverage across all units, complex integration test suites, full-time QA processes, staging environments with extensive smoke tests on each deploy. Often accompanied by heavy CI pipelines gating every change.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | *Solo Constraint:* Writing and maintaining tests for every scenario is a huge time sink for one person. Aiming for enterprise-level coverage means you’ll spend more time updating tests than adding features (especially as the product is pivoting). Long test suites or complex CI slow down deployment significantly – not ideal when agility is your advantage. Also, many areas (UI polish, minor features) can be verified quicker manually at small scale. Over-testing too early can lead to **flaky tests and false security** [\[ruchitsuthar.com\]](https://ruchitsuthar.com/blog/software-craftsmanship/testing-strategies-startup-to-enterprise), [\[ruchitsuthar.com\]](https://ruchitsuthar.com/blog/software-craftsmanship/testing-strategies-startup-to-enterprise), without truly reducing risk in proportion to effort.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | **Heuristics:** For an MVP or prototype, cover only mission-critical paths (e.g. sign-up, purchase flow) with automated tests – that might be \~20% coverage focusing on what “breaks the business” [\[ruchitsuthar.com\]](https://ruchitsuthar.com/blog/software-craftsmanship/testing-strategies-startup-to-enterprise). If a bug in a given component would only mildly inconvenience you or a handful of users, you likely can ship without exhaustive tests there. As user count and code complexity grow, gradually increase test coverage, especially before major launches. Always ask: *“If this breaks, how bad is it?”* If the answer is “not very,” it might not need an automated test right now. Conversely, if you’re writing a medical or finance app (high risk), even a solo dev should invest in more testing – adjust for domain criticality.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| **Observability**                    | **Basic Monitoring & Logs**. Set up minimal alerts (e.g. uptime ping, error rate monitoring) and use application logs for debugging [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces). You can log to a file or use a simple hosted service for errors (like Sentry for exceptions). Focus on a few key metrics (CPU, response time) – enough to know if the app is up and performing reasonably [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces). Add more when incidents show a blind spot. | **“Three Pillars” Observability Stack** – Full **logs + metrics + distributed tracing** with systems like ELK/Graylog, Prometheus/Grafana dashboards for everything, and tracing with Jaeger or OpenTelemetry instrumentation on all services [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces). Often adopted because it’s best practice at scale, but overkill for a simple app. | *Solo Constraint:* Operating a full observability stack can feel like a second application to maintain [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces). You’ll be tweaking dashboards, storage, and trace instrumentation instead of coding features. For a single-service app, traces add little value – you know where the code runs. Excess logs and metrics without a team to analyze them = noise and cost. Small teams that **collect all three signals too early** often drown in data, paying for storage without clear insights [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces). The cognitive load of parsing a plethora of graphs and traces isn’t worth it when a simple error log often pinpoints issues. | **Heuristics:** If your architecture is a monolith or a few components, you likely only need uptime alerts, a handful of metrics, and error logs. Introduce more sophisticated tracing **only** when you truly have a complex distributed system (multiple services, message queues, etc.) where figuring out “where a request failed” becomes non-trivial [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces). As a solo dev, favor tools that are cheap (or free) and managed – e.g. use a platform’s built-in monitoring or a simple cloud log service – rather than running your own ElasticSearch cluster. Expand observability in response to pain: one day you might need deep tracing, but wait until an incident demands it rather than preemptively spending weeks on setup [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces). |
| **Background Jobs & Data Pipelines** | **Minimal Orchestration**. Use simple methods for async work: language built-ins (threads, `async/await`), or a single-process task queue (e.g. a basic **Celery/RQ** worker with Redis, or a cron job for nightly tasks). Keep the pipeline simple: a script or function for each scheduled task, triggered by cron or a lightweight scheduler. For data processing, use managed services (like a SaaS ETL) or one-off scripts as needed.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | **Enterprise Workflow Engines** – e.g. adopting **Apache Airflow, Temporal/Cadence, or AWS Step Functions** to manage sequences; building a full CI data pipeline; using **feature stores and streaming buses** for every data update. Essentially, overusing tools meant for dozens of interdependent jobs and teams.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | *Solo Constraint:* Heavy workflow systems (Airflow, etc.) require constant babysitting (configuring DAGs, workers, monitoring failures) and add complexity in deployment. If only a couple of tasks exist, it’s far simpler to handle them with code. Similarly, feature stores (like Feast) demand infrastructure and are only worth it if you have many models sharing features – in a one-dev scenario, you can just compute features on read or use a SQL table [\[ruchitsuthar.com\]](https://ruchitsuthar.com/blog/software-craftsmanship/testing-strategies-startup-to-enterprise). Over-engineered pipelines tie you down: every data schema change becomes a big deal, and you risk “pipeline break” issues that are hard to debug alone.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | **Heuristics:** **Count your tasks and models.** If you have just a handful of background jobs (email sender, report generator) or one ML model, you don’t need a specialized orchestrator or feature store – a cron plus a script or a basic queue will do. Use a full workflow engine only if you’re dealing with dozens of jobs with complex dependencies or need guaranteed execution even after crashes. For most prototypes and single-developer SaaS apps, that threshold is never reached. The moment you find yourself thinking about maintaining state between many asynchronous steps, or repeatedly re-running jobs by hand, then consider a tool – but only the smallest that fits (e.g. Cloud Functions or simple schedulers) before jumping to something like Airflow.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| **AI/LLM Model Hosting**             | **Use Hosted APIs / Smaller Models**. Leverage existing AI services: OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, or lightweight open-source models on manageable infrastructure (like running a 7B-13B model on a single cloud VM if needed). This offloads heavy lifting – you get scalability and optimization out of the box [\[techbuddies.io\]](https://www.techbuddies.io/2026/01/26/inside-openais-postgresql-strategy-scaling-a-single-primary-to-800-million-users/). For many use cases, prompt engineering on a powerful base model is sufficient; exhaust that before considering customization.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | **Self-hosting large models or fine-tuning early**. E.g. spinning up your own 30B+ parameter model cluster, or rushing into fine-tuning a proprietary model on a small dataset, because you think you need absolute control or want to avoid API costs.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | *Solo Constraint:* Managing large models is a huge ops task – obtaining GPUs, handling model serving, dealing with memory and scaling. It’s an entire infra project (even many startups avoid it initially). Fine-tuning adds long experiments, data pipelines, and hyperparameter tuning – likely overkill if you haven’t first tried prompt engineering or smaller adaptations. The cost and time can be immense for questionable gain at small scale. Meanwhile, an API would let you deliver features to users immediately.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | **Heuristics:** Use pre-trained models and APIs to validate your product idea; only when you hit a clear limitation (e.g. needing offline processing, extreme latency concerns, or proprietary data can’t go to a third-party) should self-hosting come on the table. Even then, start with the smallest model that meets your needs – you can always scale up to bigger models or proper MLOps when user demand and resources justify it. Remember OpenAI’s example: they serve *800M users on a single Postgres* and let their hosted API handle the ML heavy work [\[techbuddies.io\]](https://www.techbuddies.io/2026/01/26/inside-openais-postgresql-strategy-scaling-a-single-primary-to-800-million-users/). That’s leverage you likely need as a solo dev.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| **LLM Orchestration**                | **Straightforward Calls & Logic**. Treat LLMs as a service: make direct API calls (or use a minimal SDK) to get completions, and handle any necessary post-processing in your code. If your application requires multiple steps, implement them with normal code or scripts calling the LLM sequentially – you drive the process. Use few, well-chosen tools/functions for the LLM, or none at all, to minimize confusion.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | **Complex agent frameworks & toolchains**. Using things like **LangChain with multi-step agents** for simple tasks, or wiring up **AutoGPT-like multi-agent loops** that recursively call themselves. Equipping LLMs with dozens of tools by default. In general, handing control to an “AI orchestrator” to decide workflow and tool use, instead of explicitly coding the sequence.                                                                                                                                                                                     | *Solo Constraint:* These frameworks can obscure what’s happening and are hard to debug when the output is wrong [\[developers...redhat.com\]](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2026/02/17/uncomfortable-truth-about-vibe-coding). They also tend to be resource-heavy (e.g. extra API calls, bigger memory usage) for little benefit in simple applications [\[developers...redhat.com\]](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2026/02/17/uncomfortable-truth-about-vibe-coding). As a one-person team, you want determinism and clarity; complex chains reduce both, making it challenging to troubleshoot why your AI did X instead of Y. Giving an LLM 20 tools “just in case” can degrade performance (Anthropic: >15 tools hurts accuracy) [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81). You become essentially a spectator to an unpredictable AI process, which is risky without a squad of prompt engineers.                                                                                                                       | **Heuristics:** If your use of LLMs can be described in a simple flowchart, implement it directly rather than using a generic agent framework. Only consider an autonomous agent approach if the problem truly requires open-ended exploration (which is uncommon in business apps). When using an LLM, start with **zero tools** – add one or two only if you find the model truly can’t solve the task without them. If you ever think *“Why is this agent doing weird stuff?”*, that’s a sign the approach might be too complex. Favor simple `call -> get result -> use result` patterns at small scale.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| **LLM Data Management (RAG)**        | **Simple Embeddings & Local Store**. For Retrieval-Augmented Generation, start with a basic setup: use a embedding API (OpenAI or open-source models) to vectorize content, and store vectors in a local index (FAISS, or a lightweight vector extension for PostgreSQL/SQLite) [\[techitez.org\]](https://techitez.org/ai/vector-db-101-wordpress-rag-pinecone-sqlite-faiss/), [\[techitez.org\]](https://techitez.org/ai/vector-db-101-wordpress-rag-pinecone-sqlite-faiss/). This is often enough for tens of thousands of documents. Manage data updates with simple scripts or on-demand recomputation.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | **Standalone Vector DB / Realtime sync**. Spinning up a **vector database service (Pinecone, Weaviate, etc.)** from the start, and building real-time data ingestion pipelines into it. This may include stream processing, message queues, or using a full search engine (Elasticsearch, Solr) for even modest data sizes.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | *Solo Constraint:* Hosted vector DBs incur monthly costs and complexity (API keys, network latency) that aren’t justified for small projects. Self-managing one means running another server or cluster. The benefit (fast similarity search) is negligible if your data is small (e.g. a few hundred MB or less) – local methods can handle that in-memory. The pipeline overhead of keeping a vector DB in sync (with messaging, etc.) is also overkill if your knowledge base changes infrequently; a simple cron job or on-demand re-index may do.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | **Heuristics:** **Size matters:** if your entire corpus is under a few hundred MB or a couple hundred thousand entries, you can likely use an embedded solution with millisecond query times [\[techitez.org\]](https://techitez.org/ai/vector-db-101-wordpress-rag-pinecone-sqlite-faiss/). Use a full vector DB service only if you’re nearing a million+ embeddings or need to serve a high query rate concurrently [\[techitez.org\]](https://techitez.org/ai/vector-db-101-wordpress-rag-pinecone-sqlite-faiss/). Similarly, only implement continuous ingestion if data must be indexed in real-time. In early RAG prototypes, it’s fine to run a re-index script periodically or even update a JSON/SQLite store manually after content changes. Grow into more complex solutions when the data volume or update frequency truly demands it.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| **DevOps & CI/CD**                   | **Scripted Deploys & Basic CI**. Use simple deploy methods: e.g. GitHub Actions or a small shell script to build and push your app to production. Leverage managed CI (GitHub/Bitbucket CI) for running tests or Docker builds, with minimal steps. Manual approvals or feature flags can handle gating releases if needed.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | **Full CI/CD Pipeline with Environments**. E.g. setting up Jenkins/TeamCity with elaborate pipelines, multi-stage deploys (dev/staging/prod) with automated integration tests and canary releases on each merge. In small teams, this often yields more overhead than value, but AI tools might generate it by default.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | *Solo Constraint:* Maintaining a heavyweight CI/CD system is like maintaining another project. Jenkins servers, complex YAML pipelines, etc., require attention and can break (then you’re debugging your build system rather than your app). For one developer, a simpler push-to-deploy or one environment (production) is usually enough early on. Over-engineered pipelines slow development – every small change goes through excessive ceremony, when you could often test quickly in a local or staging environment manually.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | **Heuristics:** If you’re deploying multiple times a day and have users expecting zero downtime, some CI/CD automation is helpful – otherwise, manual or basic scripted deploys are fine. In a one-person team, you likely know when to deploy and can do it carefully without strict gates. As you add more team members or start getting large user influx, then invest in robust CI/CD (with review apps, staging tests, etc.). Until then, prefer simple solutions (e.g. auto-deploy main branch to production or use a single staging environment you test on). Remember, **GitHub started with deployments via a single script** – you can too, until scaling up.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |

**Key point:** For each row above, the **solo-dev choice prioritizes simplicity, speed, and manageable scope**, whereas the enterprise alternative optimizes for large scale, teams, or strict reliability. A solo developer should bias toward solutions that require **the least maintenance and configuration**, because you have no one to delegate devops and overhead to. Move to more complex architectures only when your project’s success and growth absolutely demands it – and ideally when you have more hands to help.

***

## Top “Vibe Coding” Traps and Safe Defaults

Modern AI coding assistants are trained on a lot of enterprise code, so they sometimes suggest over-complicated patterns by default. Here are 7 common **AI-suggested architecture choices** that solo devs should **think twice about**, and what to do instead:

*   **AI suggests microservices for a new app** → Use a **single-module service or monolith** – you can always split later → **Don’t use:** multiple service repos, gRPC-by-default, service meshes (Istio), or separate datastores per microservice for a one-dev project. [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html)
*   **AI suggests container/Kubernetes deployment** (for a simple app) → **Deploy on one server or PaaS** (docker if needed, but no K8s) → **Don’t use:** Kubernetes, Mesos, Istio, or complex Helm charts when a single Docker container or VM would do. [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749)
*   **AI suggests GraphQL/gRPC** (for a single client or internal API) → **Keep it simple with REST or direct calls** → **Don’t use:** GraphQL servers, gRPC services, or elaborate API gateways for one UI or a prototype; avoid adding Apollo/Federation without multi-team consumers.
*   **AI suggests enterprise auth** (OAuth server, Keycloak) → **Use simpler auth** (library or hosted service) → **Don’t use:** Keycloak, custom OIDC servers, complex RBAC microservices or LDAP for a handful of users – use built-in frameworks or third-party auth (Firebase, Auth0) until scale truly demands otherwise.
*   **AI suggests a vector DB** (for light RAG) → **Use local embeddings & search** (e.g. in-memory or SQLite+FAISS) → **Don’t use:** Pinecone, Weaviate, ElasticSearch or other distributed search for <100K docs – avoid extra servers and just use a lightweight library or a single Postgres with pgvector. [\[techitez.org\]](https://techitez.org/ai/vector-db-101-wordpress-rag-pinecone-sqlite-faiss/)
*   **AI strings together multiple LLM agents** (for a task one model can handle) → **Use one LLM call or a simple script** (you control the flow) → **Don’t use:** AutoGPT-style multi-agent loops, complex LangChain pipelines, or giving an LLM dozens of tools by default – it’s unstable and overkill for most apps. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81)
*   **AI rebuilds LLM integration from scratch** (e.g. manual HTTP calls, custom wrappers) → **Use official SDKs & proven libs** (OpenAI/Anthropic SDK, Transformers, etc.) – they handle retries, errors, and updates for you. → **Don’t use:** raw HTTP requests when an SDK is available, or writing a new framework for prompt templating when lightweight libraries exist – avoid reinventing the wheel while you’re solo.

Each “trap” above follows the same rule: when in doubt, **prefer the simpler solution** that you can manage yourself easily. Use AI coding assistants to speed you up – but **always apply your architecture judgment to keep the design as simple as possible** for as long as possible. Complex architectures are not a mark of success; delivering value is. As a solo developer, your time and focus are your scarcest resources. These guidelines help you invest that time where it counts – building the product – instead of operating unnecessary infrastructure.
